name: USA
summary: The USA is a diverse wine-producing country, led by California, with styles shaped by Pacific influence, fog, altitude and continental conditions inland.
examTips: California is the most important premium wine state.
Pacific influence, fog and topography are key themes in the USA.
Compare warm Napa with cooler Sonoma and coastal regions.
Also compare cool Oregon with more continental Washington State.

california.name: California
california.summary: The key US wine state, producing a wide range of wines from ripe inland styles to fresher coastal expressions.
california.styleSummary: Ripe fruit-driven wines with freshness maintained by Pacific influence.
examTips: California is the core state to remember.
Coastal influence is critical for preserving freshness.
Fog and mountain gaps are major moderating factors.

Cabernet Sauvignon.summary: A key premium red grape in California, producing full-bodied wines with black fruit, oak and structure.
Cabernet Sauvignon.style: Blackcurrant, oak spice, full body, high tannin.
examTips: Usually fuller and riper than Bordeaux.
Oak influence is often pronounced.

Zinfandel.summary: A signature Californian red grape producing ripe, high-alcohol wines with jammy fruit and spice.
Zinfandel.style: Jammy red fruit, spice, high alcohol.
examTips: High alcohol and jammy fruit are key clues.
Can resemble Primitivo genetically.

Chardonnay.summary: A major Californian white grape capable of styles from fresh citrus to rich buttery oak-aged wines.
Chardonnay.style: Ranges from fresh citrus to full buttery oak styles.
examTips: MLF and oak can create rich buttery styles.
Cooler sites give fresher expressions.

Pinot Noir.summary: A cool-site red grape producing elegant red-fruited wines in suitable Californian regions.
Pinot Noir.style: Red fruit, soft tannin, elegant in cooler areas.
examTips: Usually from cooler coastal or elevated sites.
Lighter and fresher than warm-climate reds.

premium-sparkling.name: Premium Sparkling Wine
premium-sparkling.style: Traditional method sparkling wine from cooler Californian sites with fresh citrus fruit and autolytic complexity.
premium-sparkling.keyPoint: Think cooler California sites such as Anderson Valley and Los Carneros.

california.classification: California quality context is often taught through AVAs and the contrast between warm inland zones and cooler maritime-influenced areas.
lawNotes: Napa, Sonoma and the Central Coast are key named quality areas.
Marine influence is crucial for cool-climate Chardonnay and Pinot Noir styles.
vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Mediterranean conditions usually bring abundant sunshine and relatively dry growing seasons, helping consistent ripening.
Nearby seas or cold-water influence moderate temperature and can slow ripening, especially in coastal vineyards.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Water management, shade from canopy, and choosing cooler or higher sites are important human responses in Mediterranean climates.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Choice of production method and maturation is a major human factor in shaping regional wine style.

napa-valley.name: Napa Valley
napa-valley.summary: California's flagship premium region, known for full-bodied, ripe and concentrated Cabernet Sauvignon.
napa-valley.styleSummary: Full-bodied, ripe wines with high alcohol and concentration.
examTips: Napa is the key premium Cabernet region in the USA.
Think ripe fruit, concentration and oak.
Fog and diurnal range help preserve balance.

Cabernet Sauvignon.summary: Premium Napa red producing concentrated black-fruited wines with high tannin and oak.
Cabernet Sauvignon.style: Blackcurrant, cassis, oak, full body, high tannin.
examTips: Think concentrated premium New World Cabernet.
Usually riper and fuller than Bordeaux.

Chardonnay.summary: Riper Californian Chardonnay often showing tropical fruit, MLF and oak.
Chardonnay.style: Ripe citrus, tropical fruit, often buttery and oaky.
examTips: Rich buttery style is a key clue in warmer Californian Chardonnay.

oakville.name: Oakville
oakville.summary: Prestige central Napa AVA especially associated with concentrated structured Cabernet Sauvignon.
oakville.styleSummary: Full-bodied ripe Cabernet Sauvignon with cassis fruit, oak and firm structure.
examTips: Oakville is a benchmark Napa Cabernet name.
Warm days with moderated nights help ripe but balanced styles.

Cabernet Sauvignon.summary: Premium Napa red producing concentrated black-fruited wines with high tannin and oak.
Cabernet Sauvignon.style: Blackcurrant, cassis, oak, full body, high tannin.
examTips: Think concentrated premium New World Cabernet.
Usually riper and fuller than Bordeaux.

oakville.classification: Prestige Napa sub-AVA strongly linked to benchmark Cabernet Sauvignon.

Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Regional style depends not only on climate and site, but also on deliberate human choices in the vineyard and cellar.

rutherford.name: Rutherford
rutherford.summary: Classic Napa AVA known for premium Cabernet Sauvignon and the famous dusty savory note often linked to the area.
rutherford.styleSummary: Rich Cabernet Sauvignon with black fruit, oak and firm tannin.
examTips: Rutherford is strongly tied to Napa Cabernet identity.
Think power, structure and oak.

Cabernet Sauvignon.summary: Premium Napa red producing concentrated black-fruited wines with high tannin and oak.
Cabernet Sauvignon.style: Blackcurrant, cassis, oak, full body, high tannin.
examTips: Think concentrated premium New World Cabernet.
Usually riper and fuller than Bordeaux.

stags-leap-district.name: Stags Leap District
stags-leap-district.summary: Warm Napa district producing Cabernet Sauvignon with richness but often polished tannin and perfume.
stags-leap-district.styleSummary: Concentrated Cabernet with ripe black fruit, oak and supple texture.
examTips: This is a classic Napa Cabernet name to recognize.
Expect concentration with relatively refined tannin.

Cabernet Sauvignon.summary: Premium Napa red producing concentrated black-fruited wines with high tannin and oak.
Cabernet Sauvignon.style: Blackcurrant, cassis, oak, full body, high tannin.
examTips: Think concentrated premium New World Cabernet.
Usually riper and fuller than Bordeaux.

los-carneros.name: Los Carneros
los-carneros.summary: Cool southern district spanning Napa and Sonoma, important for Chardonnay and Pinot Noir.
los-carneros.styleSummary: Cool-climate Chardonnay and Pinot Noir with freshness and restrained fruit.
examTips: Marine influence makes this cooler than much of Napa.
Think Chardonnay and Pinot Noir rather than Napa Cabernet.

Chardonnay.summary: Riper Californian Chardonnay often showing tropical fruit, MLF and oak.
Chardonnay.style: Ripe citrus, tropical fruit, often buttery and oaky.
examTips: Rich buttery style is a key clue in warmer Californian Chardonnay.

los-carneros.classification: Cool southern AVA influenced by San Pablo Bay, important for Chardonnay and Pinot Noir.

napa-valley.classification: Napa Valley AVA is the flagship California fine-wine region, especially for Cabernet Sauvignon.
lawNotes: Warm days and cooler nights help ripeness with retained structure.
Sub-AVAs are important exam context for premium Napa Cabernet and cooler southern Chardonnay/Pinot zones.

sonoma.name: Sonoma
sonoma.summary: A diverse Californian region often cooler and more balanced than Napa due to stronger Pacific influence.
sonoma.styleSummary: More balanced wines due to stronger cooling influence.
examTips: Compare Sonoma with Napa for cooling influence.
Pinot Noir and Chardonnay are especially important.

Pinot Noir.summary: Cooler-site Californian Pinot Noir with red fruit, freshness and elegance.
Pinot Noir.style: Red fruit, fresh acidity, elegant structure.
examTips: Usually fresher and more elegant than warm inland reds.

Chardonnay.summary: Versatile white grape in Sonoma ranging from fresh citrus styles to richer oak-aged wines.
Chardonnay.style: From fresh citrus to richer oaked styles.
examTips: Cooling influence usually gives more freshness than Napa examples.

russian-river-valley.name: Russian River Valley
russian-river-valley.summary: Cool Sonoma area strongly associated with premium Pinot Noir and Chardonnay.
russian-river-valley.styleSummary: Fresh elegant Pinot Noir and Chardonnay with moderate alcohol and bright acidity.
examTips: Fog and maritime influence are key here.
This is one of Sonoma's best-known cool-climate names.

Pinot Noir.summary: Cooler-site Californian Pinot Noir with red fruit, freshness and elegance.
Pinot Noir.style: Red fruit, fresh acidity, elegant structure.
examTips: Usually fresher and more elegant than warm inland reds.

Chardonnay.summary: Versatile white grape in Sonoma ranging from fresh citrus styles to richer oak-aged wines.
Chardonnay.style: From fresh citrus to richer oaked styles.

sonoma-coast.name: Sonoma Coast
sonoma-coast.summary: Cool exposed coastal area producing taut high-acid Pinot Noir and Chardonnay.
sonoma-coast.styleSummary: Tense cool-climate wines with freshness, citrus and red fruit rather than overt ripeness.
examTips: Expect stronger ocean influence and cooler conditions.
Acidity and restraint are key clues.

alexander-valley.name: Alexander Valley
alexander-valley.summary: Warmer inland Sonoma area especially important for Cabernet Sauvignon.
alexander-valley.styleSummary: Riper fuller red wines with black fruit and oak influence.

lawNotes: Russian River Valley and Sonoma Coast are key cool-climate names.

central-coast.name: Central Coast
central-coast.summary: A broad Californian region where ocean influence creates a wide range of styles from fresh to ripe.
central-coast.styleSummary: Wide range of styles depending on proximity to ocean.
examTips: The main theme is site variation.
Closer to the coast means fresher style.

Chardonnay.summary: A flexible variety here, ranging from fresh coastal styles to riper inland wines.
Chardonnay.style: Fresh to ripe styles depending on site.
examTips: Site is more important than one fixed regional style.

Pinot Noir.summary: Cooler-site red grape producing red-fruited lighter to medium-bodied wines.
Pinot Noir.style: Red fruit, light to medium body.
examTips: Think freshness and red fruit in cooler coastal examples.

monterey.name: Monterey
monterey.summary: Cool elongated coastal valley known for fresh Chardonnay and Pinot Noir.
monterey.styleSummary: Fresh bright cool-climate wines with citrus, apple and red berry character.
examTips: Cooling ocean influence is important.
Monterey is a key cool Central Coast source.

Chardonnay.summary: A flexible variety here, ranging from fresh coastal styles to riper inland wines.

santa-barbara.styleSummary: Elegant aromatic Pinot Noir and Chardonnay with good acidity.
examTips: East-west valleys are a classic climate clue.

oregon.name: Oregon
oregon.summary: Cool-climate US region best known for elegant Pinot Noir and high-acid wines.
oregon.styleSummary: Elegant, high-acid wines, especially Pinot Noir.
examTips: Oregon is most strongly linked with Pinot Noir.

Pinot Noir.summary: Cool-climate Pinot Noir showing red fruit, freshness and elegance.
Pinot Noir.style: Red cherry, raspberry, high acidity, elegant.

washington-state.name: Washington State
washington-state.summary: A more continental US region producing ripe wines with good acidity due to warm days and cool nights.
washington-state.styleSummary: Ripe fruit with good acidity due to diurnal range.
examTips: Warm days and cool nights are the key theme.
Think ripe fruit with retained freshness.

Cabernet Sauvignon.summary: Structured red grape showing ripe black fruit with good acidity and full body.
examTips: Usually balances ripe fruit with better freshness than very hot regions.

Merlot.summary: A major Washington red grape producing plum-fruited, medium to full-bodied wines.
Merlot.style: Plum, soft tannin, medium to full body.
examTips: Usually softer and rounder than Cabernet Sauvignon.

columbia-valley.name: Columbia Valley
columbia-valley.summary: Large warm dry Washington region known for ripe Bordeaux varieties supported by irrigation.
columbia-valley.styleSummary: Ripe fruit with freshness retained by cool nights, often in Cabernet and Merlot.
examTips: Irrigation is essential in much of Washington State.
Warm days and cool nights are key style drivers.

Cabernet Sauvignon.summary: Structured red grape showing ripe black fruit with good acidity and full body.
examTips: Usually balances ripe fruit with better freshness than very hot regions.

Merlot.summary: A major Washington red grape producing plum-fruited, medium to full-bodied wines.
Merlot.style: Plum, soft tannin, medium to full body.
examTips: Usually softer and rounder than Cabernet Sauvignon.

columbia-valley.classification: Large warm dry AVA producing ripe Bordeaux-variety wines with retained freshness from cool nights.
vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.

Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.

Growers rely on site selection and exposure to exploit shelter and reduce climatic risk.
Irrigation management can be critical where natural rainfall is low or meltwater is available.

walla-walla-valley.summary: Prestige Washington AVA producing concentrated red wines from Bordeaux varieties and Syrah.

walla-walla-valley.classification: Prestige Washington AVA known for concentrated structured reds.
vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Mountain influence or rain-shadow effects can reduce rainfall and create cooler nights or sheltered mesoclimates.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Growers rely on site selection and exposure to exploit shelter and reduce climatic risk.
Irrigation management can be critical where natural rainfall is low or meltwater is available.

washington-state.classification: Washington quality context centers on warm continental AVAs, irrigation and large day-night temperature swings.
lawNotes: Irrigation is essential in much of eastern Washington.
Columbia Valley is the key broad quality AVA to recognize.
vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
